meta.verification_method: main-agent WebFetch (previous subagent sandbox denied web tools)
meta.note: Every claim below cites a fetched URL. Any cell lacking a fetched-evidence URL is tagged 'Unknown — not advertised'.

targets.data_source: monitors 'over 10 billion GitHub events in real time' — implies GH Archive / events firehose at warehouse scale (TiDB per public PingCAP docs, not re-verified in this pass)
targets.cadence: 'real time' is advertised on the landing page; specific cadence (seconds vs minutes) is not stated

ai_agents_evidence.metrics_tracked_quote: stars, pull requests, issues — last 28 days and month-to-month comparisons, plus year-to-year historical rankings since 2011
ai_agents_evidence.ranking_indicator_quote: moving up or down the rankings

targets.they_have_we_dont: Curated AI Agent Frameworks collection (17 repos, stars/PRs/issues × 28d/MoM/YoY) with live rank-change indicators. Warehouse-depth historical data back to 2011.
targets.we_could_have_they_dont: MCP server + SSE live subscribe channel + alert delivery. OSSInsight is read-only analytics; a warehouse architecture cannot push sub-minute events to an agent without re-architecting.

targets.data_source: Unknown — not advertised on landing page; self-described as 'An alternative to GitHub Trending, using a consistent scoring algorithm informed by daily engagement metrics'
targets.cadence: Daily — landing header dates page '2026-04-18', nav reads 'Trending // Daily' and 'Daily explore'
categories: Trending topics list including #AI agent, #AI coding assistant, #AI skills, and others

ai_agents_evidence.topic_position: top of the trending topics list

targets.they_have_we_dont: First-class topic taxonomy exposed as URL paths (#ai-agent, #ai-coding-assistant, #ai-skills) with daily editorial-feeling curation and aggregated topic-level star counts.
targets.we_could_have_they_dont: Agent-native subscribe surface (MCP + SSE), API, CLI, per-repo compound signals (quiet-killer, rank-climber). Trendshift is a human-browsing surface — no dev API advertised on landing.

targets.data_source: GitHub's internal trending algorithm (opaque, not documented)
targets.cadence: Today / This week / This month windows; no explicit refresh cadence stated on the page
categories: Time: Today / This week / This month

ai_agents_evidence.note: No dedicated AI or AI-agents filter. Page acknowledged AI-heavy content via the trending list itself (openai-agents-python, omi, evolver, thunderbolt observed in the list), but no category selector for AI.

targets.they_have_we_dont: Default brand. Every developer on Earth can discover trending repos at github.com/trending without a single click of onboarding. Language-by-language filter depth StarScreener can't match.
targets.we_could_have_they_dont: An AI category, alerts, API, MCP, compound signals, latency better than hours/day, badges. Everything except reach.

targets.data_source: Unknown — not explicitly stated on landing page
targets.cadence: Unknown — not stated; star histories are snapshot-rendered on demand

ai_agents_evidence.note: Not primarily a categorizing tool — product centers on per-repo star-curve rendering.

alerts.email: Unknown — 'Subscribe' string visible on landing but channel not specified in fetched content

targets.they_have_we_dont: The viral chart itself — a single iconic star-history image that gets embedded in README files and tweets. Brand-defining visual.
targets.we_could_have_they_dont: A trending screener with compound signals, categories, alerts, and API. star-history is a viewer, not a screener — they solve a different problem.
targets.scope_note: Per the brief: this is primarily a per-repo chart viewer, NOT a trending screener. The weekly trending leaderboard is a secondary surface.

targets.data_source: N/A — domain no longer hosts the product
targets.dead_site_detail: 301 Moved Permanently to writinggoals.com/about-us — an unrelated 'writing goals' site. The GitLogs product as described in the audit is defunct at this URL.
targets.they_have_we_dont: N/A — site is dead; no competitive claim possible.

targets.classification: reference tool, NOT a competitor — open-source library + self-hostable app
targets.data_source: GitHub API — both GraphQL and REST endpoints, user-provided PAT (5000/hr auth vs 60/hr unauth)
targets.cadence: On-demand per repo; initial fetch for a 100k-star repo takes ~3 minutes
targets.distinctive_capability: DUAL-ENDED FETCH WORKAROUND FOR THE 40K-STAR CAP — 'fetches star history from both ends simultaneously' to reconstruct complete daily star data for repos above GitHub's 400-page stargazer listing cap. Direct solution to StarScreener's #4 finding (296/309 flat sparklines).
targets.license_check_required: confirm MIT/Apache before porting the technique
targets.they_have_we_dont: Working 40k-cap bypass technique (dual-ended pagination) — SHIP-READY IN PRODUCTION. Exports as CSV/JSON. Self-hostable with Docker.
targets.we_could_have_they_dont: Everything trending/category/alert-related — they don't screen, they visualize. StarScreener should PORT the dual-ended fetch and keep the rest of its surface area.

moat_matrix.star_history: Subscribe string + Chrome ext; details unclear

verdict_summary: StarScreener's 'AI focus' differentiator is NO LONGER UNIQUE — OSSInsight ships a curated 17-repo AI Agent Frameworks collection with stars/PRs/issues × 28d/MoM/YoY historical ranks, and Trendshift has #ai-agent as a first-class topic with aggregated star counts. Both predate StarScreener. The moat must shift to (a) agent-native delivery (MCP + SSE + webhook for alerts — competitors don't advertise any alert surface), (b) sub-minute latency on a curated AI watchlist via GitHub Events firehose, and (c) porting daily-stars-explorer's dual-ended fetch to solve the 40k cap that blinds 296/309 seeded repos today.
